text:
I don't have a complete construction or a clean hard-regime countermodel, but the structural shape of the bound tells you a lot.

**The cost form is target-side.** $R_1 E_y + E_y^2/S_1$ is sharper than the source FF profile $E_y^{3/2}$ by exactly $\alpha^{1/2}$ where $\alpha = R_1/S_1 \ll 1$. The appearance of the target dimension $S_1$ in a source filling estimate is the key tell: no purely source-side push of $Z_y$ can give this — it has to factor through a target-side step that operates on $G_\# Z_y$ (whose uncancelled mass is $\int J_2 G \le E_y$) and is pulled back.

**Proposed framework.** Rather than constructing $H$ and $P$ separately, build the competitor as target-FF pulled back through a $G$-section, plus a flap correction:

(i) Cubulate the target plane $[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]$ at scale $\sqrt{E_y/S_1}$.

(ii) Choose a measurable section $\sigma_y$ of $G|_{Z_y}$ on the **good** set (cells whose all-coordinate plaques avoid $z$). The decisive claim: the cellular puncture criterion *is exactly* the criterion for unambiguous local sheet count of $G|_{Z_y}$. Plaques missing $z$ means no $G$-sheet-structure jump locally; the section exists cellularly.

(iii) The $R_1 E_y$ term arises as the source push of the *target-side* deformation of $G_\#Z_y$ in the $x_1$-direction, pulled back via $\sigma_y$; the cost is weighted by $J_2 G$ rather than ambient mass — this is exactly the mechanism that avoids the flap cost.

(iv) The $E_y^2/S_1$ term: residual 2D FF in $[0,S_1]\times[0,S_2]$ of the target-null part. (The 2D FF profile in a rectangle of short side $S_1$ gives $A^2/S_1$.)

(v) Flap = where $\sigma_y$ jumps = where the puncture criterion fails. Cost: $\Mass(B_{y,\theta} \llcorner \mathcal{B}^{\rm cell}_{z,\theta})$.

**The genuine obstacle.** The real content of (13c) is showing that the $G$-section's branch locus is concentrated in the all-plaque puncture-bad set. The "if" direction is plausible (missing $z$ on every plaque → no sheet jump); the "enough overlap" direction is what makes the absorbing fraction $R_3R_4/(S_3S_4)$ correct. Everything else is bookkeeping. I have not verified that the mass count in (iii) actually works out — the section pullback can have $1/\lambda$-stretching that needs to be tamed cellularly.

**On hard-regime countermodels.** A countermodel needs a bona fide $\Dil_2 \le 1$ degree-1 pair map in the extreme hard regime, and very few are known. Your exponent stress models saturate all known scalar inequalities but aren't realized by maps. My guess: no such map exists (and thus the theorem holds), but proving non-existence almost certainly requires (13c) itself.

**Concrete test I would run.** Prove the 2D analog cleanly first. $f:R^2\to S^2$ degree 1, $\Dil_1\le1$, $r_1<s_1$, 1D slices of $f_2$, energy $E_y=\int_{Z_y}|df_1|^2$. The 2D version has minimal seam/branch structure; if the cellular comparison fails there, the framework is wrong in general; if it works, it gives you the template for which steps need extra care in 4D.

**Pointers:**
- Wenger, *Filling functions of currents in Banach spaces*, GAFA 2005/2008 — filling profiles via measurable selection.
- Almgren, *$Q$-valued functions* memoir, §1 — branch locus and sheet decomposition for degree-$Q$ currents, formalizing your section construction.
- Guth, *Volumes of balls in large Riemannian manifolds*, Annals 2011 — weighted Federer–Fleming with weight $\sim 1/\lambda^2$; closest existing template I know.
- White, *Rectifiability of flat chains*, Annals 1999 — chain-level FF infrastructure.

Uncertain on whether the $R_1\int J_2 G$ accounting in (iii) actually gives $R_1 E_y$ on the nose; that's the first thing I'd verify in the 2D toy.
